items.prompt: This section asks a different question than Section 2.3. Section 2.4 focuses on:
choices: Who or what executes the chosen method
How to label images for machine learning
items.explain: Section 2.3 selects the method; Section 2.4 addresses manual versus automated execution of that method.

items.prompt: In Example 2.8, why might manual aisle audits beat ceiling cameras for the pilot?
choices: Associates can note temporary displays and damaged packaging that cameras misclassify
Manual entry eliminates all human error
items.explain: Manual collection buys contextual judgment at the cost of scale and staffing dependence.

items.prompt: Automated collection eliminates the need for alert thresholds and missing-data checks.
items.explain: Example 2.9 shows that high-frequency sensor streams still require thresholds and packet checks before they are trusted.

items.prompt: Which pairing best matches typical use?
choices: Manual — continuous API polling every second
Automated — ethnographic field notes in a small clinic
Manual — interviews and shelf audits; automated — IoT logs and scheduled scrapers
Automated — only when no software exists
items.explain: Manual fits precision and context; automation fits scale and continuous feeds.

items.prompt: Name one strength and one limit of automated collection.
items.rubric: Strength: scale/continuity/reduced human entry error; limit: setup cost/maintenance/overload/privacy.
items.sample_answer: Automation scales and runs continuously, but requires setup, maintenance, and careful retention rules to avoid drowning in noise.
